author: @JBradHarris
text:
I've scrolled through most of the comments and I haven't seen anyone point out that you've also created a continuously variable transmission with potentially more efficiency than anything in the market, and the ability to handle WAY more torque than the belt driven designs.

Could be worth a patent.

author: @CubeKnot
text: For the monopole, it looks like they used the one axis spherical gear as a cutter/hole and rolled it around a cylinder of appropriate size, that of a 16 tooth gear in the same module (maybe slightly larger to capture the curved surface?). I'm not familiar with tinkercad, but maybe it would be possible to use that stepping process you used to create the original gear. Instead of trying to orbit the spherical gear and also rotate it, it might be easier to keep both parts fixed in place at the mesh distance of the two gears and rotate them against each other. The spherical gear should have a step size of half the monopole/cylinder and it should be in the opposite direction (eg. +0.1 deg for the spherical and -0.2 deg for the monopole). Great video and I hope this is at least a little helpful!

author: @pj_ytmt-123
text:
Yeah it's an awesome design but there are two potential problems with it.

The two driving arms must be put on swivels or else the ball will lock up; that makes controlling the direction exponentially more expensive computationally.

If the ball ever 'slipped' it would be a nightmare to reset its orientation, as it is (*just < )360 deg. in three dimensions. 🤓

author: @jonathancard4466
text: I sort-of made a monopole gear in Fusion 360. I created a gear outline, then a cylinder, then learned to script Fusion 360 in Python. It stepped in 0.1 degrees steps (or something) and rotated the cylinder around its axis a bit, and also rotated the cylinder around the center of the gear a bit, the did a 360 revolution of the outline, cutting out the outline from the cylinder. Basically digital gear hobbing. It did about 20 steps and it worked great. I tried to go all the way around the cylinder, and Fusion locked up all night and didn’t finish. Probably broke it, not that it was still running. Sorry I can’t give more detail, the script isn’t in front of me. If you’re interested, I can dig it up.

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text:
In solid modeling tools, if I had trouble replicating a shape that meshed with another shape, I'd try using inverse area then unrolling it so it's like a big flat driving plane (in this case) and some creative shading and connecting the dots (maybe ink the one to see if it leaves voids on the other to add back in).
I bet using this method one could make another whole ball the same size and if the designs could be converged maybe the joint could be built out of two identical balls, halving the parts count of the BOM.

author: @icebluscorpion
text: Use Solidedge Community edition and you get it with a few clicks even the monopongear. Why using the wheelchair of tinkercad to get up the stairs when the (equivalent of Fusion 360) Solidedge Community edition skyrocketed you up the stairs 😅. That program is absolutely free for non Comercial people and has no restrictions besides of non commercial use only. Hack you can even run all simulations which fusions 360 restricted it for payers only. If someone asks I gladly recommend Solidedge Community edition. Robert, You asked I answered. Keep it up pal you are awesome 💪! The learning curve is steep as in fusion 360 though but you get even free workshops/tutorials. And can get a certificate. The strategy of Siemens is if the program is free to learn/master then the person whom will eventually go to business, then they will be more willing to pay for the professional one, where they get more services targeted for commercial use. And I love this strategy. Because I get my overpowered CAD/CAM/circuit design/simulation program. For my projects and can then show a certificate like ECDL for Siemens Solidedge.

author: @gerryplayz4532
text: I think you'd only be able to get a monopole if it was half the circumference of the sphere. With the sphere set as a hole you could align it with the half sized cylinder, move it in to match the thread depth, then rotate each 1° counter-clockwise and merge, I don't know if the repeat function in tinkercad can repeat more than one action at a time, if it can do the turn-turn-merge all good, if not you could do it with that programming tool you showed a while back. There's also macros that some people use for games that can copy your clicks, you just hit record then click away, hit stop, put in how many times you want it to auto-repeat, press play and away it goes! Great video btw 😎👍

author: @scotttovey
text:
The interesting thing about that ball gear, is that it can be used in many applications such as a joy stick.

Rather than having a friction driven system, it would be driven by the gears and make for less loss of functionality as happens when friction drives begin too wear or get dirty.

You would of course, have to size it properly and print it in material that has a long wear life.

replies.author: @PneumaticFrog
replies.text: @scotttovey magnets would be superior and more reliable

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@PneumaticFrog 
"magnets would be superior and more reliable"

I don't see the point in using magnets in this application.

I guess you can get really small one's which will be necessary in order to turn the variable resistors in the joy stick. 
But will they be strong enough to do the job?

Most variable resisters used for joy sticks do not have a linear motion, they have a circular motion.

If  you want optimum reliability and functionality, the best solution would be to gut a working track ball, then design a body and stick you can mount the components into, and glue the ball into the stick.

Then you would want to connect it to an Arduino or other microprocessor that converts the mouse signals into joystick motion.
On the other hand, if you are real handy in the area of firmware development, you can build a joystick using track ball components making sure the device can be easily opened up for cleaning.

replies.author: @distantstate
replies.text: I concur. I’d never have thought this was something you could do in Tinkercad.

author: @jeremyboyce7921
text: This is fantastic. I saw that demonstration video a while ago and thought that the mechanism has lots of potential uses.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is an interesting mechanism and now you canmake your own

replies.author: @genekoesling
replies.text: @jeremyboyce7921 agreed! The first thing I wondered when I saw how it moves was whether it can be precise enough and strong enough to work as a camera or telescope mount.

author: @joelsoncdma
text: Top! Like

author: @katiemmm347
text: crazy what you can create in Tinkercad thanks again for another master class

replies.author: @PunakiviAddikti
replies.text: I've always used tinkercad to make complex shapes. It's intuitive. I tried to learn fusion and make a box that's 20mm x 20mm but I just couldn't do it. It was a mess. So I gave up and just used tinkercad for all my 3D printing.

replies.author: @xmysef4920
replies.text: @PunakiviAddikti This. People always hate on tinkercad for being a ”childs tool” when you can do a BUNCH of things in it
